title: Needle felting a base shape
subtitle: Building compact, stable felt with a barbed needle and foam pad
excerpt: Needle felting uses a barbed needle stabbed repeatedly into dry roving to tangle the fibres together without water. This foundational technique produces the compact base shape that dimensional needle-felted figures, brooches, and decorative elements are built from.
sourceNotes: Needle felting as a home craft developed from the industrial needle-felting machines used in the textile industry to produce non-woven fabrics. The domestic hand-needle method became widely established in craft circles from the 1980s onward; the technique is observation-led rather than book-cited, as the primary literature predating 1928 does not document dry needle felting for domestic use.

content.text: Needle felting compacts dry roving into a dense, stable mass by driving a
barbed needle

repeatedly through the fibre. Each downstroke drags fibres from the surface layer into the interior, where they tangle with other fibres and lock in place. The result is a compact shape that can be moulded, layered, built up, and refined to any level of detail. A base shape is the solid inner core: once it is dense enough that the needle no longer sinks in easily, the base is ready for surface colour work and detailing. Carded fleece prepared as a
rolag
compacts well too; allow a few extra minutes at the start as the lofty fibres bed in.

attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.
attrs.title: Needle safety

content.text: What you need
Method
Step 1: Form a rough ball or disc of roving

Pull off a length of roving about 40 cm long. Fold it in half, then in half again, and roll it loosely between your palms into a rough ball. For a disc or flat oval, fold and press the roving into the target shape by hand before needling. The rougher the starting shape, the more needling time the finish takes, but the fibre does not need to be tidy at this stage: any looseness gets driven into the core during the first few minutes of needling.
Step 2: Begin needling the surface
Place the rough form on the
foam pad

. Hold the needle in your writing hand with a loose grip about halfway up the shaft. Stab straight down through the surface of the form into the foam, then pull the needle straight back out. Work over the whole surface of the form in a grid pattern: five or six stabs per square centimetre, moving methodically across the top face. After 2-3 minutes, turn the form over and work the opposite face.
Step 3: Rotate and needle all faces

Rotate the form every few minutes and work each face and edge in turn. This even working is what produces a consistently compact shape rather than a hard top with a soft core. As the form compacts, it becomes noticeably smaller and denser. Loose wisps of fibre on the surface can be tucked in or needled down; larger tufts can be pulled off and re-incorporated by placing them against the surface and needling them in.
Step 4: Density test

A properly compacted base shape resists the needle: driving it in requires noticeably more pressure than at the start of the session. If the needle slides in with little resistance, the form is not dense enough to hold surface detail without collapsing. Continue needling until the resistance is even across the whole surface. For a 20 g base shape, this typically takes 20-30 minutes of steady working.
Step 5: Refine the surface

Switch to a finer needle (size 40) for the surface pass. Work the needle shallowly across the surface, about 3-5 mm deep rather than the full shaft length. This brings stray fibres to the surface and smooths the finish. For a textured surface (like a bird's body with visible feather texture), skip the fine-needle pass and leave the slightly fuzzy surface from the size 38 needle.
Where this technique goes

A firm base shape is the start for 3D needle-felted figures (robins, animals, plant forms), flat brooches and patches, and mixed-media felted pictures. Add surface colour and detail once the base is fully firm. Press small pieces of coloured roving against the surface. Needle them in with a fine needle.

attrs.intro: Three failure modes in needle felting base shapes.

items.fix: Always stab at a strict 90-degree angle to the surface. If working a curved surface, rotate the form rather than tilting the needle.
items.cause: The needle was entering or leaving the roving at an angle. Angled stabs hit the foam pad edge-on instead of tip-first and bend the shaft.
items.symptom: Needle bends or tip breaks during stabbing

items.fix: Use a longer needle stroke, driving the needle its full length into the form. Increase the stab density across the surface and spend more time on each face before rotating.
items.cause: The needling has only compacted the surface layer, not the interior.
items.symptom: Form feels firm on the outside but soft in the core when squeezed

items.fix: Continue working. The loose fibres are the unincorporated exterior of the form migrating inward. After 15 minutes, the surface should be substantially cleaner.
items.cause: The roving still has a lot of loft; this is normal in the first 10 minutes of needling.
items.symptom: Loose fibres keep appearing on the surface as you needle

attrs.heading: Common problems
